narrative_blocks.header: Lost his mother at age seven
narrative_blocks.body: Born 1905 in Pittsburgh PA, eldest son of Pictorialist photographer Bernard Shea Horne Sr. and WWGA champion golfer Bessie Anthony. Lost his mother in May 1912 at age seven; raised by his father (who did not remarry) and his Horne dept store family.

narrative_blocks.header: Compiled an Izaak Walton Compleat Angler bibliography
narrative_blocks.body: He authored a scholarly bibliography of editions of Izaak Walton's 'The Compleat Angler' (1653) — one of the most-printed books in the English language, with hundreds of editions across nearly four centuries. The bibliography established him as a serious bibliophile and angling-literature collector.

notable.notable_blurb: Pictorialist photographer in MoMA Walther Collection

parents.mother_name: Mary Elizabeth Shea Horne — Joseph Otto Horne's 1st wife and sister of business partner Christian Bernard Shea; her brother's name passed down as her son's middle name 'Shea.'
parents.mother_research_notes: Mary Elizabeth Shea Horne — Joseph Otto Horne's 1st wife; sister of business partner Christian Bernard Shea ; her brother's name passed down through her son as Bernard Shea Horne Sr.

cross_connections.display_label: Co-orbit artist: American Symbolist painter also entered tree through marriage
a fellow pictorialist photographer of the same era

narrative_blocks.header: Named for his maternal uncle and business partner
narrative_blocks.body: Born 1867 to Pittsburgh dept store founder Joseph Otto Horne and his first wife Mary Elizabeth Shea Horne. His middle name 'Shea' came from his maternal uncle Christian Bernard Shea — Joseph's business partner and co-founder of the Joseph Horne Company. The name carried both maternal Shea lineage and the family-business heritage forward.

narrative_blocks.header: Pictorialist at Clarence H. White School
narrative_blocks.body: He studied photography at the Clarence H. White School of Photography — the most influential American Pictorialist institution of the 1910s-20s — alongside Max Weber, Paul Strand, and other major figures. The school trained an extraordinary number of artists who later became known beyond Pictorialism: Margaret Bourke-White, Dorothea Lange, Anton Bruehl.

narrative_blocks.header: MoMA Walther Collection holds his prints
narrative_blocks.body: His Pictorialist photographs — soft-focus, painterly, often philosophical in subject — are preserved in the Museum of Modern Art's Walther Collection, alongside other late Pictorialists. He was personally close with Max Weber, who is among the most important early American modernists.

Around 1903 he married Mary Elizabeth 'Bessie' Anthony of Chicago, who was then in the middle of winning the Women's Western Golf Association championship four years in a row. They had two sons (1905 + 1909) before her early death at 32 in May 1912 left him a widower at 44.

sources.label: Museum of Modern Art Walther Collection
Clarence H. White School of Photography records

cross_connections.display_label: Distant cousin and fellow Hooker descendant Olympic-tier woman athlete
was her father-in-law; Pittsburgh dept store founder, 1849

narrative_blocks.body: Beginning at age 20 in 1901, she won the Women's Western Golf Association championship — one of the two major US women's tournaments — four years in a row through 1904. Remains one of the few WWGA champions to win four consecutive years, dominant in an era of evolving women's golf in America.

narrative_blocks.header: Married a Pittsburgh dept store photographer
narrative_blocks.body: Around 1903 — during the height of her golf dominance — she married Bernard Shea Horne Sr. of the Pittsburgh Horne dept store family. He was a serious Pictorialist photographer studying with Clarence H. White; her family wealth combined with the Horne dept store fortune made the marriage consequential socially as well as artistically.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at thirty-two leaving two young sons
narrative_blocks.body: She died at Pittsburgh in May 1912 at age 32 — only eight years after her fourth WWGA championship and just seven years into her marriage. Left two young sons: Bernard Jr (7) and Joseph Galway Horne (3), who would carry the Anthony-Horne line into the next generation and join via Joseph's marriage to the Lovejoy Carnegie Steel family.

sources.label: Women's Western Golf Association historical records 1901-1904

parents.father_name: John Horn (his son rendered the surname 'Horne')

career.notes: Founded 1849 as dry goods business; expanded into full dept store. One of the earliest US dept stores.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded one of America's earliest department stores
narrative_blocks.body: In 1849 at age 23 he opened a dry goods business in Pittsburgh PA. Over the next 43 years he expanded it into a full department store — one of the earliest such institutions in the United States. The Joseph Horne Company remained a Pittsburgh fixture for 145 years before its 1994 closure.

narrative_blocks.header: His 1893 building became a Pittsburgh landmark
narrative_blocks.body: The Joseph Horne Building (1893-94, designed by William S. Fraser) on Penn Avenue Pittsburgh became one of the city's commercial-architecture landmarks. After the store closed in 1994 the building was demolished 2005, but the site is now Penn Avenue Place — a major Pittsburgh office complex bearing the store's historic name.

narrative_blocks.header: Grandfather of a Pittsburgh photography pioneer
narrative_blocks.body: Through his grandson Bernard Shea Horne Sr (1867-1933) — a Pictorialist photographer who studied at the Clarence H. White School and whose prints are in MoMA's Walther Collection — the Horne business dynasty connected to the American photography world. The grandson's marriage to Bessie Anthony of Chicago brought the dynasty into the Hooker descendant tree.

narrative_blocks.header: Shea + Burchfield partners intermarried into the dynasty
narrative_blocks.body: He joined forces with Christian B. Shea — whose sister Mary Elizabeth he married — and A.P. Burchfield, whose families intermarried with the Hornes and entered the business. Family-business consolidation defined the dept store's first half-century: brothers, brothers-in-law, sons, and nephews all worked under the Horne name.

After his uncle's father-in-law Rev. John Ripley Adams had served as a teacher there in 1821-22, and 40 years after John Adams's 22-year principalship ended, he graduated Phillips Academy Andover in 1875 — first alphabetically on his class list. He went on to Union College of Law Chicago in 1878.

He was fifteen when the Great Chicago Fire swept the city 8-11 October 1871. His father Judge Elliott Anthony saved approximately 1,500 books by burying them in his garden; most of his illustrated books and encyclopedias were burned. He carried these memories into his own 60-year Chicago legal career.

narrative_blocks.header: Father of America's first 4-time golf champion
narrative_blocks.body: He married Mary P. Green in September 1879 in Chicago; they had four daughters. Eldest Bessie won the Women's Western Golf Association championship four years in a row 1901-1904 — making her one of the most dominant early American women's sports figures before her 1912 death at 32.

cross_connections.display_label: Second cousin: fellow Pope Dwight grandchild + Phillips Academy alumnus

institutions.notes: Class of 1875; first alphabetically on class list.

education.notes: First alphabetically on class list. ~40 years after John Adams's principalship ended; ~50 years after his uncle Rev. Edward Strong Dwight's father-in-law Civil War chaplain John Ripley Adams taught there.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of America's first 4-time WWGA champion
narrative_blocks.body: She married Charles Elliott Anthony in September 1879 in Chicago. They had four daughters in 9 years — Bessie (1880, the future Women's Western Golf Association champion 1901-04), Miriam (1881), Eleanor (1883), and Grace (1888). Two of her daughters died prematurely in their 30s — Bessie at 32 in 1912 + Eleanor at 39 in 1922.
